what_went_right: Worktree-first and test-before-touch discipline held end to end: the agent refused to write code on main ('You're on main… Run /work… or say skip worktree', transcript ln 1102), then took a green baseline (186/186) on the fresh worktree before touching anything (ln 1322).

The work-agent self-spotted the Critical onDocClick document-listener leak BEFORE the background reviewer returned (ln 1390: 'One thing I already spotted that the reviewer will likely flag: the onDocClick document listener leaks…').

It did not race the reviewer: while the reviewer was reading app.js the agent explicitly held all edits ('no racing on app.js', ln 1402) and folded its own catch plus the 2 should-fixes and 1 nit into a single post-review pass before one commit.

The dynamic-library-removal risk was de-risked by READING the fetch path instead of assuming: pickSuggestedReactions only reads suggested/frequently_used keys, so the live library was 'a popularity hint, never a validation whitelist' and exposing 392 names 'doesn't change the risk profile at all' (ln 1325).

Multi-file build protocol followed: background code-reviewer + background test-writer spawned per file; tests grew 186 → 211 → 212 green with all review findings (1 Critical, 2 Should-fix, 1 Nit on commit 2; 1 Should-fix, 1 Nit on commit 1) resolved before commit.

Dead-code removal was clean and intentional: dropping the dynamic lib killed ensureReactionLibrary, suggestedReactions, _reactionLibrary, and three now-unused imports, with pickSuggestedReactions kept exported+tested in compose.js (ln 1378, diff app.js:4857-4914).
The reviewer's test-gap should-fix was honored with a test for the REAL call-site shape: a Map iterator (state.comments.values()), not just plain arrays (test/picker.test.mjs ln 715-726).
what_went_wrong: Genuinely thin — this was a clean ship. The one open thread: the live-API spot-check that Substack accepts all ~392 obscure reaction names was correctly identified as 'the one real risk' and put in the test plan as a MANUAL step, but it had NOT been executed at retro time (ln 1492, 1348). The analytical conclusion ('the old path was never a whitelist') is necessary but not sufficient to close an empirical risk.

Minor process friction (not a code defect): the retro receipt's prior state was stale (.review/last-verdict.json carried a main-branch sha 499ecf4 before being overwritten to feat/emoji-picker-copy), a known artifact of the verdict-file-as-mutable-state design rather than a mistake on this branch.
three_whys: Why 1: The picker shipped exposing ~392 POST-able reaction names with the accept-all-names assumption verified only by reasoning, not by a live POST. — Because the agent read the fetch path, correctly proved the dynamic library was a popularity hint and never a validation whitelist, and concluded the risk profile was unchanged, which downgraded the live probe from a blocker to a 'manual spot-check during QA' (ln 1325).

Why 2: That downgrade was reasonable but left an empirical gap open. — Because 'the old path wasn't a gate' answers whether the CHANGE introduces new risk; it does NOT answer whether the pre-existing optimistic-POST behavior actually succeeds for obscure names. Substack accepting thumbs_up tells you nothing about it accepting a rare Objects/Symbols name; the only way to know is to POST one and watch it stick after the next poll.

Why 3: The gap stayed open at retro time because the verification was a MANUAL in-browser step that the automated test suite structurally cannot cover (no live Substack POST in vitest), and the agent deferred it to the user's QA pass rather than executing it before the retro — appropriate sequencing, but it means the receipt must explicitly carry the open spot-check forward as an action item so it isn't silently dropped after merge.

evidence_citations.quote: One thing I already spotted that the reviewer will likely flag: the `onDocClick` document listener leaks on the toggle-close and open-another-picker paths (the top-of-function querySelectorAll(...).remove() nukes the DOM node but never removes its document listener).
I'm holding edits until the reviewer returns (no racing on app.js). When it lands I'll fold its findings plus the onDocClick listener-leak fix I already spotted into one pass, then commit 2.

let activePickerClose = null; ... if (activePickerClose) activePickerClose(); ... document.removeEventListener("click", onDocClick, true); if (activePickerClose === closeThisPicker) activePickerClose = null;

Settled the risk: pickSuggestedReactions only reads suggested/frequently_used keys — the live library was a popularity hint, never a validation whitelist. ... exposing all 392 names doesn't change the risk profile at all — it's a pre-existing property. The probe drops from blocker to a quick manual spot-check during QA.

The one real risk — spot-check: post 3-4 obscure emojis (Objects/Symbols section), confirm they stick after the next poll. This verifies Substack accepts arbitrary reaction names from the static catalog (it was never validated against a whitelist — pre-existing property, but worth a live check before tag).
export const filterReactionsByQuery = (query) => { const all = Object.entries(REACTION_EMOJI); ... }

action_items.title: Run the live spot-check that Substack accepts obscure reaction names before tagging the picker
action_items.description: In a logged-in browser with the extension loaded, open the picker and POST 3-4 obscure names from the Objects/Symbols/Weather sections of REACTION_EMOJI (not the common thumbs_up/fire set). Confirm each reaction sticks after the next poll cycle rather than optimistic-bumping then silently rolling back. If any name is rejected, Substack is whitelisting — intersect the static catalog with the accepted set (or filter at render) so the picker never offers a dead-click. This is the empirical close-condition for the static-catalog-exposes-untested-reaction-names learning; the analytical 'the old path was never a whitelist' conclusion does not substitute for it. Route to the BetterSSC narrative in ~/.claude/roadmap.md as a pre-tag QA gate.
